module-1-course-enrollment-directory.label: Module 1 Project: Course Enrollment Directory
module-1-course-enrollment-directory.summary: Build a department-to-student enrollment directory through five cumulative multi-table steps.

sketch0.title: A Directory Built Across Four Tables

step_1.title: Connect Courses to Departments
step_1.prompt: Begin the Module 1 Course Enrollment Directory. Return `courses.title, departments.name` from `courses` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`.
step_1.hint: This is the first project step. Build a clean starting version, then run it before moving on.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Return `courses.title, departments.name` from `courses` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`.

step_1.solutionCode: SELECT courses.title, departments.name FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id;

step_2.title: Add Enrollment Records
step_2.prompt: Extend the Module 1 Course Enrollment Directory with the next approved requirement. Return `courses.title, departments.name, enrollments.term` from `courses` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `courses.id = enrollments.course_id`.
step_2.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

help.concept: Read the brief as a result contract: identify the source table or join path, the result columns, the row condition or grouping rule, and any required ordering.
help.hint_1: Build the result one clause at a time and run it after each meaningful change.
help.hint_2: Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `courses.id = enrollments.course_id`.

step_2.starterCode:
SELECT courses.title, departments.name FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id;
-- Project step 2: Add Enrollment Records
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Extend the query to include enrollments so each result row represents one enrollment in a course
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step_2.solutionCode: SELECT courses.title, departments.name, enrollments.term FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id INNER JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id;

main_py.content:
SELECT courses.title, departments.name FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id;

-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Extend the query to include enrollments so each result row represents one enrollment in a course
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
SELECT courses.title, departments.name, enrollments.term FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id INNER JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id;

step_3.title: Add Student Details
step_3.prompt: Extend the Module 1 Course Enrollment Directory with the next approved requirement. Return `courses.title, departments.name, students.name, students.grade_level, enrollments.term` from `courses` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `courses.id = enrollments.course_id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `students` on `enrollments.student_id = students.id`.

step_4.title: Clarify the Directory Output
step_4.prompt: Extend the Module 1 Course Enrollment Directory with the next approved requirement. Return `departments.name AS department, courses.title AS course, students.name AS student, students.grade_level AS grade_level, enrollments.term AS term` from `courses` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `courses.id = enrollments.course_id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `students` on `enrollments.student_id = students.id`.

step_5.title: Sort the Directory for Review
step_5.prompt: Complete the Module 1 Course Enrollment Directory. Return `departments.name AS department, courses.title AS course, students.name AS student, students.grade_level AS grade_level, enrollments.term AS term` from `courses` in that column order. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `departments` on `courses.department_id = departments.id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `enrollments` on `courses.id = enrollments.course_id`. Use a INNER JOIN to connect `students` on `enrollments.student_id = students.id`. Sort the final result by `department ASC, course ASC, student ASC`.
step_5.hint: Begin with the working code from the previous step. Keep that code, follow the new comments, and add only the next focused behavior.

step_5.starterCode:
SELECT departments.name AS department, courses.title AS course, students.name AS student, students.grade_level AS grade_level, enrollments.term AS term FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id INNER JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id INNER JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id;
-- Project step 5: Sort the Directory for Review
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Order the results by department, course, and student in ascending order for a clear review
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.
step_5.solutionCode: SELECT departments.name AS department, courses.title AS course, students.name AS student, students.grade_level AS grade_level, enrollments.term AS term FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id INNER JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id INNER JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id ORDER BY department ASC, course ASC, student ASC;

main_py.content:
SELECT departments.name AS department, courses.title AS course, students.name AS student, students.grade_level AS grade_level, enrollments.term AS term FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id INNER JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id INNER JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id;
-- Project step 5: Sort the Directory for Review
-- Keep the working code above from the previous step.
-- Next, Order the results by department, course, and student in ascending order for a clear review
-- Add only the focused change for this step inside the existing work.

SELECT departments.name AS department, courses.title AS course, students.name AS student, students.grade_level AS grade_level, enrollments.term AS term FROM courses INNER JOIN departments ON courses.department_id = departments.id INNER JOIN enrollments ON courses.id = enrollments.course_id INNER JOIN students ON enrollments.student_id = students.id ORDER BY department ASC, course ASC, student ASC;

sketch-1.title: Project brief: course enrollment directory
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
The academic office needs a directory that can answer a complete question in one row: **which student is enrolled in which course, and which department offers that course?** The values are spread across four tables, so the report must follow the full path `departments → courses → enrollments → students` without skipping a relationship.

Keep the grain at **one row per enrollment**. That choice allows a course to appear once for every enrolled student and allows a student to appear once for every course taken. The finished directory should use readable output headings, retain the enrollment term, and sort the result so departments, courses, and students are easy to scan.

Build the path cumulatively. Begin with the course and its department, cross the enrollment bridge, then reach the student. At each step, preserve the working query and verify that the new join enriches the same enrollment row rather than changing its meaning. The final SQL should read like a traceable route through the ERD, not a collection of unrelated conditions.
